Mr. President, Commissioner, honourable colleagues, it is neither my intention nor that of my group, which has supported and requested this debate, to transfer matters of Italian internal policy to the European level.

The issue of freedom of information concerns us all; it concerns the European Union, which can and must regulate the matter at the community level, in order to counter political and economic pressures and ensure genuine pluralism in information.

The Italian anomaly, where even owners of some of the most significant private networks also hold substantial media, political, and economic power, lies precisely in the dangerous intertwining of media, political, and economic powers.

It is precisely for this reason that we call for a European regulatory intervention, an intervention that far from being an act of anti-Italian sentiment, aims to safeguard the pluralism of information, preventing concentrations and conflicts of interest.

Madam President, honourable colleagues, the proposal on passengers' rights for bus travel aims to improve and protect passengers, particularly disabled passengers or those with reduced mobility.
This is the first regulation on bus passengers' rights and should ensure passengers, as is already the case in the rail and air sectors.
The position in second reading aims to establish a set of rights for passengers who choose to travel by bus.

I agree with the rapporteur Cancian’s position, whom I thank for the excellent work carried out, and I believe the scope of application of the regulation should be revised by excluding regional services when they are integrated with urban and suburban services.
It would be fair and appropriate to ensure passengers' rights in the event of accidents, cancellations, or departure delays, as already decided by the Parliament in its first reading.

Equally important is the issue of passenger rights information. It would be beneficial, therefore, to provide passengers with information regarding connections with other modes of transport. This will also foster dialogue between passenger transport services via bus and those via rail.

With regard to disabled persons and those with reduced mobility, maximum protection must be ensured, and I propose reducing the timeframe from 48 to 24 hours, in order to request assistance and obtain greater guarantees for compensation and replacement of the devices that ensure mobility for persons with disabilities in the event of loss or damage.
Moreover, taking into account the needs of passengers with reduced mobility, any architectural barriers should be removed and existing infrastructure should be improved to ensure accessibility.
This regulation aims to enhance the competitiveness of the bus sector, promote intermodality, and above all, improve passengers' travel conditions.

In many Member States, there has been a lack of political will to combat corruption. Consider that there are still European countries that have not signed the United Nations Convention against Corruption.

When considering the devastating impact of corruption on democratic processes, citizens' rights, and trust in the rule of law, it is clear that the national anti-corruption measures adopted so far by Member States are insufficient to contain a phenomenon that is expanding.

It is the European Union that must provide a unified and robust response, especially now, at the height of an unprecedented economic crisis, which is profoundly affecting people's lives, the sustainability of pension and welfare systems, and the capacity of institutions to ensure essential services.

Honourable colleagues, Finland and Estonia have been placed in a queue for council accession for a very simple reason. Bulgaria and Romania are currently unable to ensure effective border control and are plagued by rampant corruption involving both the political class and organized crime.

In the event that Bulgaria were to enter this stage at this moment, police and security agencies foresee that Bulgarian borders—particularly the maritime ones—would become easy targets for Turkish crime and for organized crime from Eastern Europe, and that Bulgaria and Romania would become crossroads for human and drug trafficking.

It is natural that some Member States have significant reservations regarding Bulgaria and Romania's actual ability to ensure effective border controls against organized crime and corruption, and it is therefore entirely natural that they have opposed their accession to Schengen.
Allowing this now would mean paving the way for transnational crime and creating a breach in European borders precisely at a time when there is intense migratory pressure from Turkey.
Many are the opposing reasons, and Europe cannot ignore them.
I therefore ask to know what intentions those who omitted to provide advice have regarding this delicate matter, which in my opinion has so far been addressed with su
sufficiency and without taking into account the risks that the accession of Bulgaria and Romania would entail, according to competent bodies dealing with security and international crime

In my view, the Commission and the Council should postpone the accession of these two countries to Schengen and grant approval only when there is reasonable certainty that the accession of Bulgaria and Romania will not affect the security of European citizens.

Madam President, honourable colleagues, the phosphates present in household laundry detergents end up in water systems, rivers, streams, and lakes across Europe.

Phosphorus triggers an acceleration in the reproduction of various forms of life. Consequently, algae and other rapidly developing plant species proliferate excessively when phosphorus levels increase in an aquatic environment.
This excessive algal growth deprives other living organisms of oxygen and may also produce toxins that destroy fish and plant species, thereby reducing the water's quality itself.
I therefore cast my vote in favor of the text adopted today, as it is desirable to reduce the amount of phosphorus entering the waters of our continent in order to purify Europe's water resources.

while we believe the true European judge is the national judge who changes his mindset and becomes imbued with an understanding of European law.

For this reason, before discussing training, it is essential to engage in extensive communication with citizens and legal professionals, so that they become advocates of this significant innovation and help foster the willingness to take advantage of the benefits offered by this right.

But above all, it must be a bottom-up movement that develops within in-service training. One cannot deliver lectures to adult, highly professional judicial personnel as if they were schoolchildren in judicial training.

One must foster curiosity when dealing with one's own cases, connect with other colleagues, and seek solutions, as today's common law and civil law systems converge and are no longer as distant from each other as they once were within the legal culture.

Sir, Commissioner, today you have asked Europe to take on a new role in coordinating industrial policies, and even more stringent advocates of autonomy and private initiative now demand the exercise of this role.
Without a greater European integration of national strategies, even in terms of employment protection, the current crisis will not be overcome and there will be no revival.
The crisis in the automotive industry is not merely one of overproduction capacity and a dramatic loss of employment.
But the crisis now appears to be structural, affecting the very fate of the car and the product as they are today.
We want to keep the automobile industry in Europe and we want to prevent the dispersal of existing expertise, know-how, and professional skills.
We therefore call for the initiation of a major restructuring and revitalization project, and not one that is rushed, because costs are proportional to the time invested.
Restructuring cannot be merely the result of fragmented national decisions or individual companies.
We must at least attempt to coordinate and steer this painful process at the community level, bringing the relevant stakeholders to the table through social dialogue.
The trade unions and national governments, by providing support for research, innovation, human capital training, requalification, and the necessary funding.
I know it will be a difficult blow because the national interests of individual companies are not aligned.

But since the current competitive struggle within the European Union is highly ambiguous and sometimes risks being unfair, I believe that, in the end, it is in the interest of all countries to have an effective community-driven impetus and leadership to jointly face the increasingly aggressive global market.

The energy crisis has imposed and continues to impose a very high cost on all employment, affecting thousands of precarious workers, as well as stable and temporary workers, who are numerous in this sector.
The major steel conglomerates are increasingly turning toward the Far East or the American continent, raising a significant question about the direction of our development model.
I believe that Europe must regain the understanding that a manufacturing system such as ours requires robust basic industries capable of supplying the European internal market.
We must therefore defend this sector and the role of stable steel production; Mr. Commissioner, if we wish to halt industrial decline, we shall support it.

And I believe we must move beyond a certain dualism that has not been reached here this evening, but that is often heard in discussions on this subject—a dualism between environmental advocates and workers' advocates—which is incorrect because it prevents us from seeing a vision for the future.
An industry that views steelmaking as an integral component requires advanced production systems and concrete, balanced solutions that safeguard diverse interests and different needs.

There is thus a strong European project underway, one that emphasizes sustainability, on which we must not retreat, in my view, as a distinctive added value with which we wish to compete globally, supporting investments as has been done.

More new technologies, new processes, and plant requalification, toward a European economy that makes effective use of resources, including energy resources, indeed starting from energy resources, and also in the search for new and different materials.

Madam President, honourable colleagues, as we have recalled in this Chamber, next December the United Nations agency responsible for information and communication technologies will convene in Dubai for the World Conference on International Communications.

a supranational structure such as the IITU, of which we know hundreds of states from around the world are members, many of which are known for issues related to democracy, transparency, and freedom of expression.

Therefore, I believe it is necessary to safeguard the role of the internet and the potential it especially holds for revitalizing economic growth, not merely that, but also to ensure opportunities for all.
I therefore hope that the Commission works toward the goal that Europe, Dubai, be united and speak with one voice.

Many controversial proposals have been presented at the negotiation table of the World Conference on International Telecommunications, particularly those suggesting changes to internet governance that challenge network neutrality and could have negative consequences for consumers.
Therefore, internet freedom—or more precisely, freedom of expression and data protection—is at risk.
As some states have proposed, this would indeed allow a government to intrude not only into the communications of its own citizens but even into the data passing through its own networks.
The step from here to the limitation of freedom of expression to censorship is extremely short, and therefore I believe it is contradictory to the fundamental principles of the European Union.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, shark finning is an environmentally unacceptable practice as it threatens the sustainability of certain shark species that are targeted for this purpose.

social and economic, and because it entails the waste of a food resource with commercial value, as well as from an ethical standpoint, since it demonstrates a complete lack of respect for animal life.

Although this practice has been prohibited since 2003 in European waters and for the European fleet, the Commission has decided to submit a proposal aimed at eliminating the exemption to the ban on removing shark fins aboard Union fishing vessels.

This proposal, by further ensuring the protection that sharks require, will enable the European Union to actively contribute to the global effort underway to eradicate the cruel practice of shark finning in other parts of the world.
